content.text: What an Azure Resource Is

An Azure resource is a manageable item available through Azure — a virtual machine, a storage account, a SQL database, a virtual network, an App Service, a Kubernetes cluster. Every resource has a type (defined by its resource provider), a name (unique within a scope), a location (Azure region), and a set of properties defined by the resource type's API schema.

Resources are the leaves of the Azure resource hierarchy: Management Group → Subscription → Resource Group → Resource. Every resource belongs to exactly one resource group and, through it, to exactly one subscription. A resource cannot exist outside a resource group.

attrs.alt: Azure resource anatomy: resource ID segments (subscription, resource group, provider, type, name) and the three key concepts (resource provider namespace, resource type, and type-specific properties)

content.text: Resource Providers

A resource provider is a service that supplies Azure resource types. Resource providers are identified by a namespace in the format Microsoft.<Service> (e.g., Microsoft.Compute, Microsoft.Network, Microsoft.Storage). Each provider exposes one or more resource types and the REST API operations (Create, Read, Update, Delete, and custom actions) for those types.

Resource providers must be registered for each Azure subscription before you can create resources of their types. Most commonly used providers are registered automatically when you first use a resource of that type. Less common providers require explicit registration.

# List all registered resource providers for the current subscription
az provider list --query "[?registrationState=='Registered'].{namespace:namespace, state:registrationState}" --output table

# List ALL providers (including not registered)
az provider list --output table

# Register a provider explicitly
az provider register --namespace Microsoft.ContainerService

# Check registration status
az provider show --namespace Microsoft.ContainerService --query registrationState -o tsv

# List all resource types for a specific provider
az provider show \
  --namespace Microsoft.Compute \
  --query "resourceTypes[].{type:resourceType, locations:locations[0]}" \
  --output table

If you receive an error like 'The subscription is not registered to use namespace Microsoft.X', the resource provider needs to be registered. Run az provider register --namespace Microsoft.X and wait a few seconds for registration to complete before retrying.
The Resource ID
Every Azure resource has a globally unique identifier called the resource ID. It encodes the full path in the resource hierarchy:
/subscriptions/{subscriptionId}/resourceGroups/{resourceGroupName}/providers/{resourceProviderNamespace}/{resourceType}/{resourceName}
For child resources (resources that belong to a parent resource, like a SQL database that belongs to a SQL server), the ID includes the parent:
/subscriptions/{subId}/resourceGroups/{rg}/providers/Microsoft.Sql/servers/{serverName}/databases/{dbName}

Resource IDs are case-insensitive but Azure returns them in the casing of the original creation request. They are stable — a resource ID never changes for the lifetime of the resource. If you move a resource to a different resource group or subscription, the resource ID changes because the path changes.

# Get the resource ID of a specific VM
az vm show \
  --resource-group myapp-prod-web \
  --name web-01 \
  --query id \
  --output tsv

# Get resource IDs for all resources in a resource group
az resource list \
  --resource-group myapp-prod-web \
  --query "[].id" \
  --output tsv

# Reference a resource by its full ID (useful in cross-group operations)
VM_ID="/subscriptions/SUB_ID/resourceGroups/myapp-prod-web/providers/Microsoft.Compute/virtualMachines/web-01"

# Get resource details by ID
az resource show --ids "$VM_ID" --output jsonc

# Add a tag to a resource by ID (no --resource-group needed)
az resource tag --ids "$VM_ID" --tags Owner="web-team" --is-incremental
API Versions and the ARM REST API

Every Azure resource type has a versioned REST API. When you use the Azure CLI, portal, or an ARM template, you specify (or the tool defaults to) an API version for the resource type. The API version determines which properties and operations are available for that resource type — newer API versions add new properties and capabilities; older API versions may not expose features added after their release date.
In ARM templates and Bicep, the API version is required for every resource definition:

# List available API versions for a resource type
az provider show \
  --namespace Microsoft.Compute \
  --query "resourceTypes[?resourceType=='virtualMachines'].apiVersions[]" \
  --output tsv

# Call the ARM REST API directly (useful for debugging and advanced operations)
az rest \
  --method GET \
  --url "https://management.azure.com/subscriptions/SUB_ID/resourceGroups/myapp-prod-web/providers/Microsoft.Compute/virtualMachines/web-01?api-version=2023-03-01" \
  --query "properties.hardwareProfile"

# List all VMs in a subscription via the REST API
az rest \
  --method GET \
  --url "https://management.azure.com/subscriptions/SUB_ID/providers/Microsoft.Compute/virtualMachines?api-version=2023-03-01" \
  --query "value[].{name:name, location:location, vmSize:properties.hardwareProfile.vmSize}" \
  --output table
Resource Properties and Configuration
Each resource type defines its own schema of properties — the configuration options available for that resource. Properties are split into:
Required at creation:
properties that must be provided when the resource is created. A VM requires hardwareProfile.vmSize, storageProfile.osDisk, and networkProfile.networkInterfaces.
Optional at creation:
properties with defaults (e.g., a storage account defaults to LRS redundancy if you do not specify the SKU).
Read-only:
properties set by Azure after creation (e.g., provisioningState, id, etag, or a VM's provisionedBy timestamp).
Updateable post-creation:
properties you can change on a running resource (e.g., VM size can be changed if the VM is deallocated; storage account SKU can be changed for certain redundancy transitions).
Immutable:
properties that cannot be changed after creation (e.g., storage account kind, SQL server name, VNet address space if subnets exist).

# Show all properties of a resource (VM example)
az vm show \
  --resource-group myapp-prod-web \
  --name web-01 \
  --output jsonc

# Show a specific property path
az vm show \
  --resource-group myapp-prod-web \
  --name web-01 \
  --query "{vmSize:hardwareProfile.vmSize, osType:storageProfile.osDisk.osType, state:powerState}" \
  --output table

# Update a VM property (change VM size — requires VM to be deallocated first)
az vm deallocate --resource-group myapp-prod-web --name web-01
az vm resize \
  --resource-group myapp-prod-web \
  --name web-01 \
  --size Standard_D4s_v3
az vm start --resource-group myapp-prod-web --name web-01

# Update a storage account SKU (change replication type)
az storage account update \
  --resource-group myapp-prod-data \
  --name mystorageaccount \
  --sku Standard_GRS
Resource Dependencies in ARM Templates

When deploying multiple resources together, some resources depend on others (a VM NIC must exist before the VM can reference it; a SQL database depends on its SQL server). ARM resolves dependencies either automatically (when you reference a resource's properties in another resource's definition) or explicitly via the dependsOn property.
Querying Resources at Scale with Azure Resource Graph

The Azure CLI's az resource list command operates within a single subscription and resource group. For queries that span all subscriptions in a tenant (find all VMs missing a backup policy, find all public IPs, count all SQL servers by region), use Azure Resource Graph.

Resource Graph is a free service that maintains a near-real-time index of all Azure resources across all subscriptions you have access to. It supports the Kusto Query Language (KQL) and returns results in seconds for queries that would take minutes if run per-subscription.

# Install the Resource Graph extension
az extension add --name resource-graph

# Find all public IP addresses across all subscriptions
az graph query -q "
  Resources
  | where type == 'microsoft.network/publicipaddresses'
  | project name, resourceGroup, subscriptionId, location,
    ipAddress = properties.ipAddress,
    allocationMethod = properties.publicIPAllocationMethod
  | order by name asc
" --output table

# Find all VMs by size and region (useful for Reserved Instance planning)
az graph query -q "
  Resources
  | where type == 'microsoft.compute/virtualmachines'
  | summarize count() by
    vmSize = tostring(properties.hardwareProfile.vmSize),
    location
  | order by count_ desc
" --output table

# Find resources missing a specific tag (compliance audit)
az graph query -q "
  Resources
  | where isnull(tags.CostCenter) or tags.CostCenter == ''
  | project name, type, resourceGroup, subscriptionId
  | order by type asc
" --output table

# Count resources by type across all subscriptions
az graph query -q "
  Resources
  | summarize count() by type
  | order by count_ desc
  | limit 20
" --output table
Extension Resources

Extension resources are a special category of Azure resources that extend the behavior or add metadata to another resource (the target resource) without being a child resource of it. Extension resources are in their own provider namespace and attach to a target resource via a scope parameter or the target resource's ID.
Common extension resources you interact with regularly:
Extension resource type
What it does
Target resource
Microsoft.Authorization/roleAssignments
Grants an RBAC role to a principal
Any resource, resource group, subscription, or management group
Microsoft.Authorization/locks
Applies a ReadOnly or CanNotDelete lock
Any resource or resource group
Microsoft.Authorization/policyAssignments
Applies an Azure Policy to a scope
Any resource, resource group, subscription, or management group
Microsoft.Insights/diagnosticSettings
Enables diagnostic logs and metrics routing to Log Analytics, Event Hub, or Storage
Most Azure resources
Microsoft.Security/assessments
Security posture assessments from Defender for Cloud
Subscription or specific resource
microsoft.alertsManagement/smartDetectorAlertRules
Application Insights smart detection rules
Application Insights components

# List all role assignments on a specific resource (extension resources attached to it)
az role assignment list \
  --scope "/subscriptions/SUB_ID/resourceGroups/myRG/providers/Microsoft.Compute/virtualMachines/web-01" \
  --include-inherited \
  --output table

# List all diagnostic settings on a resource (Microsoft.Insights/diagnosticSettings extension)
az monitor diagnostic-settings list \
  --resource "/subscriptions/SUB_ID/resourceGroups/myRG/providers/Microsoft.Compute/virtualMachines/web-01" \
  --output table

# In Bicep: attach a diagnostic setting to a VM (extension resource pattern)
# resource vmDiagnostics 'Microsoft.Insights/diagnosticSettings@2021-05-01-preview' = {
#   name: 'vmDiagnostics'
#   scope: myVM    // scope points to the target resource
#   properties: {
#     workspaceId: myLAW.id
#     metrics: [{ category: 'AllMetrics', enabled: true }]
#     logs: [{ category: 'Administrative', enabled: true }]
#   }
# }
Managed Resources and Resource Groups

Some Azure services (like Azure Kubernetes Service, Azure HDInsight, and Azure Managed Disks in certain configurations) create a secondary resource group when you deploy them. This resource group is called the managed resource group and is owned by the Azure service — you cannot delete it directly, and modifications to resources inside it may be overwritten by the service.
AKS node resource group:

when you create an AKS cluster, Azure creates a separate resource group (default name: MC_{clusterRG}_{clusterName}_{location}) that contains the VM scale sets, load balancers, public IPs, and disks for the cluster nodes. You can modify this name at creation time but not after.
Azure Databricks managed resource group:
contains the VNet, NSGs, and storage resources managed by the Databricks service.
Azure App Service Environment (ASE) resource group:
ASE creates infrastructure resources in a service-managed resource group.

# Get the AKS node resource group name
az aks show \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --name myAKSCluster \
  --query nodeResourceGroup \
  --output tsv

# Specify a custom node resource group at cluster creation
az aks create \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --name myAKSCluster \
  --node-resource-group "MC-myapp-prod-aks-nodes" \
  --location eastus \
  --node-count 3 \
  --generate-ssh-keys

# List resources in the AKS node resource group (managed by AKS — do not modify manually)
az resource list \
  --resource-group "MC-myapp-prod-aks-nodes" \
  --query "[].{name:name, type:type}" \
  --output table

Do not manually modify resources in managed resource groups. Azure services own those resources and may overwrite your changes or enter an inconsistent state. Only modify resources in managed resource groups if explicitly supported by the service documentation.
Resource Naming Constraints and Best Practices

Each Azure resource type has its own naming constraints: minimum and maximum length, allowed characters, and whether the name must be globally unique (like storage account names and public DNS names) or only unique within a resource group or subscription.
Resource type
Max length
Allowed characters
Scope of uniqueness
Virtual Machine
15 (Windows) / 64 (Linux)
Alphanumeric and hyphens (Linux allows more)
Within resource group
Storage Account
Lowercase alphanumeric only
Globally unique across all Azure (forms DNS name)
Key Vault
Alphanumeric and hyphens
Globally unique (soft-delete makes names reserved for 90 days after deletion)
Azure SQL Server
Lowercase, numbers, hyphens
Globally unique (forms DNS: {name}.database.windows.net)
Resource Group
Alphanumeric, hyphens, underscores, parentheses, periods
Within subscription
Public IP DNS label
Alphanumeric and hyphens
Globally unique per region ({label}.{region}.cloudapp.azure.com)

# Check if a storage account name is available (globally unique)
az storage account check-name \
  --name "mystorageaccount" \
  --output jsonc

# Key Vault names are soft-deleted and reserved for 90 days after deletion
# List soft-deleted Key Vaults in the subscription (can block reuse of names)
az keyvault list-deleted \
  --query "[].{name:name, location:properties.location, deletionDate:properties.deletionDate}" \
  --output table

# Purge a soft-deleted Key Vault to free the name immediately (irreversible)
az keyvault purge --name myoldkeyvault --location eastus

# Validate resource name format using Azure CLI flag
# (CLI returns error if name doesn't meet constraints)
az storage account create \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --name "My_Account_Name"  # Will fail: uppercase and underscore not allowed
  --location eastus \
  --sku Standard_LRS
Idempotency in Resource Operations

Azure Resource Manager is designed for idempotent operations: deploying the same ARM template or Bicep file multiple times produces the same result as deploying it once. This is the foundation of infrastructure-as-code reliability.

Idempotency works because ARM compares the desired state (the template) to the current state (the resource's actual properties in Azure) and only applies the delta. If the resource does not exist, it is created. If it exists with the same properties as the template, no action is taken. If it exists with different properties, only the differing properties are updated.
Immutable properties require delete + recreate:

if an immutable property (like a storage account's kind or a VNet's address space in certain cases) differs between the template and the actual resource, ARM cannot update it in-place. It will fail with an error. To change an immutable property, you must delete and recreate the resource.
PUT vs PATCH:

ARM uses PUT (full replacement of properties) for most resource updates. PATCH (partial update) is available for some resource types. The Azure CLI maps to PUT by default when updating resource properties.
Conditional operations:

ARM supports If-Match headers using ETags to implement optimistic concurrency. This prevents simultaneous deployments from overwriting each other's changes, though the Azure CLI and portal don't expose this by default.

# Idempotent resource creation: create a storage account, run it twice safely
az storage account create \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --name mystorageaccount \
  --location eastus \
  --sku Standard_LRS \
  --kind StorageV2

# Second run: ARM detects the account exists with these properties, no-ops
az storage account create \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --name mystorageaccount \
  --location eastus \
  --sku Standard_LRS \
  --kind StorageV2
# Output: provisioningState: "Succeeded" (already existed; no change applied)

# Attempting to change an immutable property fails
az storage account create \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --name mystorageaccount \
  --location eastus \
  --sku Standard_LRS \
  --kind BlobStorage  # Different kind — immutable; ARM returns an error
Summary

Azure resources are the leaf-level objects in the resource hierarchy — virtual machines, databases, storage accounts, and every other service instance. Each resource is identified by a globally unique resource ID, belongs to a resource provider namespace, has a type with a versioned REST API, and has a set of properties defined by that API.
Resource providers (Microsoft.Compute, Microsoft.Network, etc.) must be registered per subscription before their resource types can be used.
The resource ID encodes the full hierarchy path: subscription → resource group → provider → type → name.
API versions in ARM templates and Bicep determine which properties and operations are available — always check the latest stable API version when authoring new templates.
Properties are either required at creation, optional with defaults, read-only, updateable post-creation, or immutable — knowing which category a property falls into prevents failed updates.
ARM and Bicep infer resource dependencies automatically when you reference one resource's output in another; use dependsOn only when there is no property reference to drive the ordering.
Azure Resource Graph enables tenant-wide KQL queries across all subscriptions for inventory, compliance audits, and cost analysis.
